mcqs.question: What is the primary advantage of using a dynamic array to implement a queue?

options.text: Efficient use of memory with no overhead
No overflow, as the array can grow dynamically
Simplified implementation with no resizing needed

mcqs.explanation: The primary advantage of using a dynamic array to implement a queue is that it can grow dynamically, preventing overflow and allowing the queue to accommodate any number of elements.

mcqs.question: When a dynamic array-based queue is full and a new element is enqueued, what typically happens?

options.text: The array capacity is doubled and elements are copied
An error is thrown and the element is rejected
A linked list node is created instead

mcqs.explanation: When a dynamic array-based queue is full and a new element is enqueued, the array capacity is typically doubled, and the existing elements are copied to the new array.

mcqs.question: What is the amortized time complexity of enqueue in a queue implemented using a dynamic array with a doubling strategy?
mcqs.explanation: Although individual resizes cost O(n), the doubling strategy ensures that the total cost of n enqueues is O(n), giving an amortized cost of O(1) per operation.

mcqs.question: In a dynamic array-based queue implementation, what is the purpose of the 'size' field?

options.text: To keep track of the number of elements in the queue
To store the capacity of the dynamic array
To maintain the front and rear indices of the queue
To implement the doubling strategy for resizing

mcqs.explanation: The 'size' field is used to keep track of the number of elements currently in the queue, which helps in checking for full or empty conditions and in implementing the doubling strategy.

mcqs.question: What is the worst-case space utilization of a dynamic array that doubles its size?
mcqs.explanation: Right after a doubling, the array is half full and half empty. So in the worst case, up to 50% of the allocated capacity may be unused.

mcqs.question: Why is shrinking a dynamic array at half capacity (size = capacity/2) problematic?

options.text: It leads to thrashing -- repeated grow and shrink cycles

mcqs.explanation: If you shrink at half capacity, alternating enqueue and dequeue operations near that boundary trigger repeated resizes (grow then shrink), destroying amortized performance. Shrinking at capacity/4 avoids this.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the 'front' and 'rear' indices in a dynamic array-based queue implementation?

options.text: To implement the doubling strategy for resizing
To maintain the order of elements in the queue
To keep track of the number of elements in the queue
To manage the circular nature of the queue

mcqs.explanation: The 'front' and 'rear' indices are used to manage the circular nature of the queue, ensuring that elements are added and removed correctly.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the dequeue operation in a dynamic array-based queue implementation?
mcqs.explanation: The dequeue operation in a dynamic array-based queue implementation has a time complexity of O(1), as it only involves updating the 'front' index and returning the element at that position.
